articles.title: Niger - Perspectives sur la sécurité alimentaire : Les conflits se prolongent avec des résultats de Crise (Phase 3 de l’IPC) dans les zones affectées, Février - Septembre 2026
articles.description:
Country: Niger
Source: Famine Early Warning System Network

Please refer to the attached file.

Messages clé

  • Dans les régions de Tillabéry et Diffa et le nord-ouest de la région de Tahoua, l’insécurité alimentaire aiguë de Crise (Phase 3 de l’IPC) persiste avec quelques ménages en Urgence (Phase 4 de l’IPC). Les conflits liés aux groupes armés terroristes, qui s’étendent à Dosso, continuent de perturber les activités de production agricole et pastorale ainsi que les circuits d’approvisionnement et le fonctionnement des marchés. Les ménages pauvres résidents et les personnes déplacées internes (PDI) sont les plus affectés. Les ménages victimes des inondations répétées à partir de juin 2026 seront également parmi les plus affectés.
  • Des résultats de Stress (Phase 2 de l’IPC) sont anticipés dans les zones agricoles, agropastorales et pastorales non affectées par les conflits avec des proportions de plus en plus élevées de personnes affectées entre juin et septembre 2026. Le stock céréalier des ménages pauvres sera épuisé et ils vont dépendre des achats sur les marchés pour leurs nourritures. Cependant, avec ces achats, l’accès des ménages pauvres sera limité en raison des prix élevés et du faible pouvoir d’achat, qui s’est érodé suite à la baisse des salaires de la main-d’œuvre agricole. Toutefois, dans les zones pastorales, l’amélioration des conditions pastorales à partir de juillet 2026 va améliorer l’état d’embonpoint des animaux, leur productivité et leur valeur marchande, renforçant ainsi l’accès aux aliments et aux revenus pour les ménages éleveurs.
  • FEWS NET estime que 2,5 à 2,9 millions de personnes auront besoin d’assistance alimentaire, et le maximum sera atteint entre juillet et septembre 2026. La population la plus préoccupante est constituée par les PDIs et les ménages pauvres et très pauvres dans les zones affectées par le conflit et dont les stocks alimentaires sont précocement épuisés.
  • Les conflits et l’insécurité dus aux activités des groupes armés terroristes perdurent, avec une tendance à l’expansion territoriale à Dosso. La région de Tillabéry demeure l’épicentre des foyers de tension, avec une hausse de 23 pour cent des incidents sécuritaires entre 2024 et 2025. Cependant, les incidents ont fortement augmenté, de plus de 200 pour cent, à Dosso durant la même période.

L’analyse présentée ici est basée sur les informations disponibles au 18 février 2026.

articles.title: Zimbabwe - Food Security Outlook: Excessive rains and dry spell likely to drive localized 2026 harvest reductions, February - September 2026
articles.description:
Country: Zimbabwe
Source: Famine Early Warning System Network

Please refer to the attached file.

Key Messages

  • In typical deficit-producing areas in the south, east, west and far north, Crisis (IPC Phase 3) outcomes are expected during February and March (the lean season peak) due to depleted food stocks among poor households and increased market reliance. Most deficit-producing areas will likely improve to Stressed (IPC Phase 2) with the April/May harvest.

  • In surplus-producing areas mainly in the Mashonaland Provinces, Stressed (IPC Phase 2) outcomes are expected between February and March due to limited availability of food stocks and constrained seasonal income. The April/May harvest and income from crop sales and other typical sources are expected to result in Minimal (IPC Phase 1) outcomes through the remainder of the outlook period. However, production deficits in some areas will likely result in localized Stressed (IPC Phase 2) outcomes.

  • The April/May harvest is expected to be near average. An early start to the 2025/26 rainfall season and sustained rains countrywide resulted in above-average area planted and favorable cropping conditions. However, the excessive rains in January were followed by a dry spell from late January to end of February, resulting in widespread moisture stress. Some households in worst-affected areas lost some of their crops due to moisture stress, and reduced yields will offer only short-lived improvements in food security, especially among poor households.

    The analysis in this report is based on information available as of February 20, 2026.

articles.title: World: Global Price Watch January 2026 Prices (February 28, 2026)
articles.description:
Country: World
Source: Famine Early Warning System Network

Please refer to the attached file.

Key Messages

  • In West Africa, staple food prices across the Sahel were stable or declined in January, reflecting post-harvest availability and weak household demand. Prices were markedly below 2025 levels. In Nigeria, cereal prices declined, driven by harvest supply, foreign exchange stability, and import waivers. In the Central African Republic, prices remained elevated above last year and the five-year average, particularly in insecure zones, driven by insecurity, high fuel costs, and logistical constraints (Page 3).
  • In East Africa, staple food prices were generally stable in Burundi, Ethiopia, South Sudan, Sudan, and Tanzania, supported by improved availability and subdued market demand. Prices increased in Kenya and Somalia due to tight supply and elevated demand for both household consumption and livestock feed. In conflict-affected areas of Sudan, prices remained stable but at exceptionally high levels, while in South Sudan prices were volatile amid rising market disruptions (Page 4).
  • In Southern Africa, maize grain and maize meal prices in January continued their downward annual trend, with white maize in South Africa nearly 49 percent below last year. However, lean-season dynamics drove monthly price increases in Zambia and Mozambique as household stocks depleted. A 41 percent fuel price increase in Malawi compounded affordability pressures despite declining food inflation across the region. Persistent foreign exchange constraints in Malawi limited import capacity for food, fuel, and fertilizer (Page 5).
  • In Central America, total white maize supply remained close to average, and prices declined compared to the previous month, due to improved seasonal availability. Red and black bean prices were stable or marginally declined, supported by increased availability from recent harvests. In Haiti, market activity remained constrained in Artibonite, Central Plateau, and Port-au-Prince due to protracted insecurity. Local food prices were generally stable or showed slight seasonal increases. In Venezuela, monthly inflation eased in January but remained significantly above last year, reflecting continued exchange rate depreciation. Prices in local currency rose sharply from 2025, while prices in USD remained stable due to improved purchasing power in foreign currency (Page 6).
  • In the Middle East, Europe, and Asia, staple food price stability in Afghanistan was supported by currency appreciation and steady imports. Relative stability prevailed in Lebanon and IRG-controlled areas of Yemen, supported by exchange rate stability, while more volatile or upward movements were observed in disruption-affected and segmented markets. In Syria, the West Bank, and Gaza, national averages masked persistent fragmentation. Staple prices moderated in Syria despite continued regional disparities, while in Gaza prices stabilized below late-2025 peaks but many remained above pre-crisis levels. In the West Bank CPI rose 1.04 percent month-on-month amid movement restrictions. In SBA-controlled areas of Yemen, food price increases were reported, though price monitoring data remain unavailable (Page 7).
  • International markets showed mixed trends in January, with maize prices broadly stable as record global production offset firm export demand, while rice prices rose 2 percent on firmer demand but remained well below last year’s levels due to ample Indian exports. Wheat prices declined marginally on record global supply and competitive Black Sea and Argentine exports. Crude oil prices increased slightly amid weather-related supply disruptions and geopolitical tensions. Nitrogen fertilizer prices continued to climb, with urea up 17 percent year-over-year (Page 2).

Recommended citation: FEWS NET. Global Price Watch February 1, 2026: February 2026 Price Watch, 2026.

articles.title: Journal of Catastrophe Risk and Resilience
articles.description: The Journal of Catastrophe Risk and Resilience (JCRR), is a diamond open-access interdisciplinary research journal with a broad remit encompassing natural hazards, catastrophe risk, and the societal and institutional response to their management.

articles.title: Our work and what we do
articles.description: UNDRR is the lead UN entity for the coordination of disaster risk reduction. We help decision makers across the globe better understand and act on risk.

articles.title: HNPW 2026: Strengthening early warning systems in fragile, conflict and violence-affected contexts
articles.description: At HNPW 2026, UNDRR, together with WMO, ITU, IFRC and IOM, will convene an online briefing to explore how early warning and early action can be strengthened in FCV contexts.

articles.title: We can – and must – harness the power of AI to tackle the complexity of disaster risk
articles.description: Kamal Kishore reflects on the future of disaster risk reduction – a future challenged by growing complexity, but made navigable by new AI-enabled capabilities. We must make the right choices now so that we use AI not only to do more, but to do better.
